[1943](/wiki/1943_NCAA_basketball_tournament "1943 NCAA basketball tournament"), [1947](/wiki/1947_NCAA_basketball_tournament "1947 NCAA basketball tournament"), [2003](/wiki/2003_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2003 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament")

infobox.value: | Away jersey    Team colours  **Away** | Alternate jersey    Team colours  **Alternate** | | |

represents the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [intercollegiate men's basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") competition. The Longhorns competed in the [Big 12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference") through the 2023–24 season and moved to the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference") (SEC) on July 1, 2024.

The **Texas Longhorns men's basketball team** represents the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [intercollegiate men's basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") competition. The Longhorns competed in the [Big 12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference") through the 2023–24 season and moved to the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference") (SEC) on July 1, 2024.

The University of Texas began [varsity](/wiki/Varsity_team "Varsity team") intercollegiate competition in men's basketball in 1906.[[2]](#cite_note-Timeline-2) The Longhorns rank 15th in total victories among all [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") programs and 23rd in all-time [win percentage](/wiki/Winning_percentage "Winning percentage") among programs with at least 60 years in Division I, with an all-time win–loss record of 1,936–1,170 (.623).[[3]](#cite_note-NCAA_Record_Book_71-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Among Southeastern Conference men's basketball programs, Texas is second only to [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball") in all-time wins and trails only Kentucky and [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas_Razorbacks_men%27s_basketball "Arkansas Razorbacks men's basketball") in all-time win percentage.[[3]](#cite_note-NCAA_Record_Book_71-3)

As of the end of the 2024–25 season, the Longhorns have won 29 total conference championships in men's basketball and have made 39 total appearances in the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") (ninth-most appearances all time, with a 40–42 overall record),[[5]](#cite_note-Tournament_history-5) reaching the [NCAA Final Four](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") three times (1943, 1947, 2003) and the [NCAA regional finals](/wiki/Elite_Eight "Elite Eight") (Elite Eight) eight times.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Fact_6-7) As of the end of the 2024–25 season, Texas ranks fifth among all [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") men's basketball programs for total NCAA Tournament games won without having won the national championship (40), tied with [Kansas State](/wiki/Kansas_State_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Kansas State Wildcats men's basketball") and [Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_men%27s_basketball "Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's basketball"), and trailing [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners_men%27s_basketball "Oklahoma Sooners men's basketball") (43), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_Fighting_Illini_men%27s_basketball "Illinois Fighting Illini men's basketball") (46), [Houston](/wiki/Houston_Cougars_men%27s_basketball "Houston Cougars men's basketball") (47) and [Purdue](/wiki/Purdue_Boilermakers_men%27s_basketball "Purdue Boilermakers men's basketball") (51).[[5]](#cite_note-Tournament_history-5)[[8]](#cite_note-2015_bracket-8)

The Texas basketball program experienced substantial success during the early decades of its existence, but its success in the modern era is of relatively recent vintage. After two losing seasons during the program's first five years, Texas suffered only one losing season from 1912 to 1950, achieving a [winning percentage](/wiki/Winning_percentage "Winning percentage") of .703 during that span, reaching two Final Fours and one Elite Eight during the first decade of the NCAA Tournament, and the [1932–33 team](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball_team "1932–33 Texas Longhorns men's basketball team") received retroactive recognition as a top-ranked team by the [Premo-Porretta Power Poll](/wiki/Premo-Porretta_Power_Poll "Premo-Porretta Power Poll"); however, the university does not claim any title, and the [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") does not recognize this designator. From 1951 to 1988, the Longhorns finished with losing records 14 times, recorded a winning percentage of .522, and participated in the expanded Tournament only five times.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Texas achieved some measure of national recognition during the tenures of [head coaches](/wiki/Head_coach "Head coach") [Abe Lemons](/wiki/Abe_Lemons "Abe Lemons") (1976–82) and [Tom Penders](/wiki/Tom_Penders "Tom Penders") (1988–98), but the program rose to its highest level of prominence under the direction of former head coach [Rick Barnes](/wiki/Rick_Barnes "Rick Barnes") (1998–2015). Barnes guided Texas to 16 [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") appearances in his 17 seasons with the program,[[11]](#cite_note-Fact_65-11) including a school-record 14 consecutive appearances (1999–2012), as well as fifteen 20-win seasons overall and a school-best 13 consecutive 20-win seasons (2000–12).[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)

Texas plays its home games in the [Moody Center](/wiki/Moody_Center "Moody Center"), which opened prior to the start of the 2022–23 season. The team is led by first-year head coach [Sean Miller](/wiki/Sean_Miller "Sean Miller"), formerly the head coach at [Xavier](/wiki/Xavier_Musketeers_men%27s_basketball "Xavier Musketeers men's basketball") and [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Arizona Wildcats men's basketball"), who was hired on March 24, 2025, following the dismissal of former head coach [Rodney Terry](/wiki/Rodney_Terry "Rodney Terry").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of Texas Longhorns men's basketball seasons](/wiki/List_of_Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball_seasons "List of Texas Longhorns men's basketball seasons")

### The early years (1906–36)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The early years (1906–36)")]

#### 1906–13

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1906–13")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/0e/UTexas_1906_basketball_team.jpg/330px-UTexas_1906_basketball_team.jpg)](/wiki/File:UTexas_1906_basketball_team.jpg)

The 1906 Texas basketball team—the university's first. Founder, player, and coach Magnus Mainland appears in third row, far left. Holding the ball is team captain C. F. von Blucher.

The Texas men's basketball program began in 1906 under the direction of Scotland native [Magnus Mainland](/wiki/Magnus_Mainland "Magnus Mainland"), a graduate engineering student and [lineman](/wiki/Lineman_(American_football) "Lineman (American football)") for the [Texas football](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_football "Texas Longhorns football") team who organized, coached, and played on the university's first varsity basketball team.[[12]](#cite_note-Little_2-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Mainland had been a nationally known basketball player as an undergraduate student at [Wheaton College](/wiki/Wheaton_College_(Illinois) "Wheaton College (Illinois)") (Illinois) prior to coming to UT.[[14]](#cite_note-A_to_Z-14) His Wheaton team placed second out of the three competing college basketball teams in the [1904 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1904_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1904 Summer Olympics") in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), the first [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") featuring the young sport (although only as a [demonstration sport](/wiki/Demonstration_sport#Summer_Olympics "Demonstration sport")).[[14]](#cite_note-A_to_Z-14) Mainland was able to persuade the University Athletic Council to set aside $125 for the preparation of an outdoor basketball court on the southwest corner of [Clark Field](/wiki/Clark_Field_(1887) "Clark Field (1887)")—the stadium then hosting the Texas football, [baseball](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_baseball "Texas Longhorns baseball"), and track teams—and to let him organize, coach, and play on the university's first varsity basketball team.[[15]](#cite_note-Penn_3-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Little_2–3-16)

The Longhorns took the court for the first time on March 10, 1906, defeating the [Baylor Bears](/wiki/Baylor_Bears_men%27s_basketball "Baylor Bears men's basketball") 27–17 on their new outdoor home court at Clark Field.[[17]](#cite_note-Fact_80-17) Texas traveled to [Waco](/wiki/Waco,_Texas "Waco, Texas") two weeks later for a three-game series with the Bears (also in their first year) and won all three games behind the play of Mainland.[[18]](#cite_note-Penn_4-18) The Longhorns ultimately won seven of the eight games scheduled in the basketball program's inaugural season.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)

Due to inadequate funding, the UT Athletic Council canceled the fledgling program after two seasons, leaving Texas without a basketball team for the 1908 season. The Athletics Council revived the program in 1909, owing in large part to the efforts of Longhorn player Morgan Vining, who campaigned to raise student interest in the game.[[2]](#cite_note-Timeline-2)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Top_100_Moments-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Vining was supported in his efforts by the UT student newspaper, *[The Daily Texan](/wiki/The_Daily_Texan "The Daily Texan")*, which consistently advocated for the reinstatement of basketball—in part because the game was viewed as good physical training for football players in the latter sport's offseason.[[22]](#cite_note-Penn_6-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/MezenthinW.png/120px-MezenthinW.png)](/wiki/File:MezenthinW.png)

W. E. Metzenthin

Language professor, German native, and Longhorn football head coach [W. E. Metzenthin](/wiki/W._E._Metzenthin "W. E. Metzenthin") (1909–11), who had argued strongly against the cancellation of basketball at UT,[[23]](#cite_note-Penn_5-23) assumed head coaching duties for three seasons following the re-establishment of the program. The Longhorns played just 10 of their 27 games under Metzenthin (and only four of their final 18) on their home court, outdoor Clark Field—with its stubbornly uneven surface and total vulnerability to weather conditions[[22]](#cite_note-Penn_6-22)—being particularly ill-suited as a basketball venue. Metzenthin finished with an overall record of 13–14; not until 1959 would another UT basketball coach leave with a losing overall record.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9) After Metzenthin relinquished coaching duties following the 1911 season in order to serve as UT Athletic Council chairman (precursor to the [athletic director](/wiki/Athletic_director "Athletic director") position), former Texas track coach [J. Burton Rix](/wiki/J._Burton_Rix "J. Burton Rix")—coaching without financial compensation, just as had his two predecessors—led Texas to a 5–1 record in his single season as head coach (1912).[[24]](#cite_note-Penn_8-24)

Professor [Carl C. Taylor](/w/index.php?title=Carl_C._Taylor_(basketball_coach)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carl C. Taylor (basketball coach) (page does not exist)"), also the Texas track coach, assumed basketball head coaching responsibilities for the 1913 season. Taylor arranged for the rental of the theater of the Ben Hur Temple and its conversion into a miniature basketball court and arena so that his team would have an indoor venue for home games and practice, with UT paying the [Shriners](/wiki/Shriners "Shriners") and [Scottish Rite](/wiki/Scottish_Rite "Scottish Rite") [Freemasons](/wiki/Freemasonry "Freemasonry") a sum of $75 for the season.[[25]](#cite_note-Scottish_Rite-25) Taylor came to UT with a strong reputation for basketball expertise, acquired during his years at [Drake University](/wiki/Drake_University "Drake University").[[26]](#cite_note-Penn_9-26) His Texas team finished with an overall record of 8–4 and, beginning with a 70–7 rout of [Southwestern](/wiki/Southwestern_University#Athletics "Southwestern University") in [San Marcos](/wiki/San_Marcos,_Texas "San Marcos, Texas"), contributed the first three victories to what would become a national-record [winning streak](/wiki/Winning_streak_(sports) "Winning streak (sports)").[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9) At the conclusion of the 1912–13 academic year, the UT's *Cactus* yearbook declared, "Basketball is no longer a minor sport at the University of Texas. It always has been so considered until this year Prof. Carl Taylor took charge of the work and infused new life into it."[[26]](#cite_note-Penn_9-26)

#### 1914–27

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1914–27")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/5e/UTexas_1915_basketball_team.jpg/500px-UTexas_1915_basketball_team.jpg)](/wiki/File:UTexas_1915_basketball_team.jpg)

L. Theo Bellmont (left) with his undefeated 1915 Texas basketball team.

[L. Theo Bellmont](/wiki/L._Theo_Bellmont "L. Theo Bellmont"), the first athletic director at The University of Texas—and a man instrumental in the formation of the [Southwest Conference](/wiki/Southwest_Conference "Southwest Conference")—took the reins as head coach from 1914 to 1915, for the first of his two stints leading the basketball team, and directed the Longhorns to 11–0 and 14–0 records in the 1914 and 1915 seasons, respectively, as well as the inaugural Southwest Conference championship during the latter season.[[17]](#cite_note-Fact_80-17)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Bellmont's teams contributed 25 victories to a winning streak that would ultimately grow to 44 games.

After his teams extended the UT winning streak to 28 games, Bellmont stepped away from coaching to focus on his work as athletic director and appointed [Roy Henderson](/w/index.php?title=Roy_Henderson_(basketball_coach)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roy Henderson (basketball coach) (page does not exist)") to the still-unpaid Texas basketball head coaching position. Henderson's team recorded Texas' third consecutive undefeated season in 1916 to extend the total to 40 consecutive victories. Senior [center](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)") [Clyde Littlefield](/wiki/Clyde_Littlefield "Clyde Littlefield"), the linchpin of the three consecutive undefeated teams[[29]](#cite_note-Penn_13-29)—and a towering figure in UT athletics history who would later coach the football team for seven seasons (1927–33), serve as the head coach of the UT track team from 1925 to 1960 (winning 25 conference championships), and found the [Texas Relays](/wiki/Texas_Relays "Texas Relays")[[30]](#cite_note-Littlefield-30)—would later receive retroactive recognition as Texas' first [consensus All-American](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Basketball_All-Americans#Consensus_teams "NCAA Men's Basketball All-Americans") in basketball for his play in the 1916 season.[[31]](#cite_note-NCAA_award_winners_2-31)

UT alumnus and former regent [Thomas Watt Gregory](/wiki/Thomas_Watt_Gregory "Thomas Watt Gregory") had begun campaigning a decade earlier for the construction of a permanent [gymnasium](/wiki/Gym "Gym") for the benefit of the student body and faculty[[32]](#cite_note-GG_History-32)—one in which the basketball team would be able to play and practice as well—but fundraising for the $75,000 project had lagged, even more so with Gregory's departure from Austin to serve as the [U.S. Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General") under [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") in 1914. The project was shelved, but the need remained acute, and, following the 1916 season, the UT Athletic Council decided to allocate $8,500 for the construction of the temporary and rudimentary all-wood Men's Gym, which was finished for the second game of the next season.[[33]](#cite_note-Penn_14–15-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e4/Eugene_Van_Gent.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eugene_Van_Gent.jpg)

Eugene Van Gent

Theo Bellmont hired [Eugene Van Gent](/wiki/Eugene_Van_Gent "Eugene Van Gent") from [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri_Tigers_men%27s_basketball "Missouri Tigers men's basketball") in 1916 to lead the Texas football, basketball, and track programs. Van Gent's single basketball team at Texas recorded a 13–3 overall mark and won the Southwest Conference championship for the third consecutive year, with the season highlighted by the first-ever basketball games between Texas and the [Texas A&M Aggies](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_Aggies_men%27s_basketball "Texas A&M Aggies men's basketball"), following the resumption of athletic relations between the two schools.[[34]](#cite_note-Penn_15-34) The Longhorns began the basketball rivalry with wins in both home games and in one of two games in [College Station](/wiki/College_Station,_Texas "College Station, Texas"). Van Gent's 1917 Texas team also added the final four wins to the Texas winning streak that had begun in 1913 before suffering a 24–18 loss to [Rice](/wiki/Rice_Owls_men%27s_basketball "Rice Owls men's basketball") in Austin.[[17]](#cite_note-Fact_80-17) Texas' winning streak stood as the NCAA record for consecutive wins in men's basketball for almost 40 years (until [Phil Woolpert](/wiki/Phil_Woolpert "Phil Woolpert")'s [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell")-led [San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_San_Francisco "University of San Francisco") teams won 60 consecutive games from 1955 to 1957), and the achievement today remains the fifth-longest winning streak in [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") history.[[20]](#cite_note-Top_100_Moments-20)[[35]](#cite_note-35) Van Gent departed after coaching for one season in each sport—winning the conference championship in both—to join the military following the United States' entry into the First World War in April 1917.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Following Van Gent's single year as head coach, Roy Henderson returned to coach Texas for two additional seasons. With several players from the 1917 team having left for military service, the 1918 Longhorn basketball team had only one returning player in sophomore Al DeViney. Henderson scouted talent in the intramural ranks and nonetheless assembled a team that finished 14–5 overall and missed winning Texas' fourth consecutive SWC championship by a single game. Henderson guided the Longhorns to a 17–3 overall record in his final season (1919), splitting the four-game series with the second-place Aggies to win the SWC championship—Texas' fourth basketball conference championship during the five years the conference had existed.[[17]](#cite_note-Fact_80-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c2/WhittakerBM.png)](/wiki/File:WhittakerBM.png)

Berry Whitaker

From 1910 through 1919, Texas recorded an overall [winning percentage](/wiki/Winning_percentage "Winning percentage") of .789.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9) Only three NCAA schools—[California](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears_men%27s_basketball "California Golden Bears men's basketball"), [Navy](/wiki/Navy_Midshipmen_men%27s_basketball "Navy Midshipmen men's basketball"), and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin_Badgers_men%27s_basketball "Wisconsin Badgers men's basketball")—achieved higher winning percentages for that decade.[[20]](#cite_note-Top_100_Moments-20)

Eugene Van Gent was set to return as head coach for the 1920 season but resigned before the beginning of the season to pursue a business opportunity in California.[[37]](#cite_note-Penn_18-37) [Berry M. Whitaker](/wiki/Berry_M._Whitaker "Berry M. Whitaker")—who had come to the university to develop and direct one of the nation's first intramural programs, and who would also coach the Texas football team for the next three seasons—agreed to serve as head coach for the season. Texas, with seven returning lettermen and war veterans who had played in 1916 and 1917, was once again expected to contend for the conference championship in 1920, but seven players missed significant parts of the season due to injury and illness.[[38]](#cite_note-Penn_18–19-38) After winning their first five games, the Longhorns lost six of eleven to finish at 10–6 overall—Texas' worst season in nine years.

Theo Bellmont designated Whitaker as the Longhorn football head coach after the departure of [Bill Juneau](/wiki/William_Juneau "William Juneau"),[[39]](#cite_note-Texas_Football:_All-Time_Head_Coaches-39) and Bellmont himself would assume basketball head coaching duties for two more seasons (1921 and 1922), finishing with a 13–5 overall record in 1921. Bellmont then led Texas to its first 20-win season during his final year. The Longhorns finished 20–4 overall and 14–4 in conference play in 1922. Missing five players to injury and grades in the latter part of the season, including the SWC's leading scorer in Phillip Peyton, Texas nonetheless entered its final game with a chance to win the SWC championship for the first time since 1919.[[40]](#cite_note-Penn_22–23-40) The Longhorns fell short in College Station against the Aggies, then coached by future Texas football head coach and Athletic Director [Dana X. Bible](/wiki/Dana_X._Bible "Dana X. Bible"), who claimed their third consecutive SWC championship.[[41]](#cite_note-Fact_105-41) Bellmont returned to his administrative responsibilities for good following the 1922 season, finishing his basketball coaching career with a 58–9 overall record; his .866 winning percentage remains the highest of any coach in program history.[[11]](#cite_note-Fact_65-11)

Bellmont selected football assistant coach [Milton Romney](/wiki/Milt_Romney "Milt Romney") as the next head basketball coach—then still an unpaid position. Romney's tenure took an early inauspicious turn when the Longhorns lost at home to [Oklahoma A&M](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Cowboys_basketball "Oklahoma State Cowboys basketball"), 28–27, after Romney called his top players to the bench with a 14-point lead and nine minutes remaining. The Longhorns again suffered a disproportionate number of injuries, including the loss of two starters to a broken leg and a fractured skull resulting from a motorcycle accident.[[42]](#cite_note-Penn_24-42) The protracted selection of [E. J. "Doc" Stewart](/wiki/E.J._Stewart "E.J. Stewart") from [Clemson University](/wiki/Clemson_University "Clemson University") as the head football coach created further turmoil and distraction when newspapers reported that he would also be charged with leading the basketball team, thus rendering Romney a lame duck with seven games to play.[[42]](#cite_note-Penn_24-42) Texas stumbled to an 11–7 finish, losing four of its final six games, but managing to close the season with a win over Bible's Aggies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/E._J._Stewart.jpg/250px-E._J._Stewart.jpg)](/wiki/File:E._J._Stewart.jpg)

E. J. "Doc" Stewart

A [medical school](/wiki/Medical_school "Medical school") graduate, a piano enthusiast, a former [sportswriter](/wiki/Sportswriter "Sportswriter"), a one-time [automobile dealership](/wiki/Car_dealership "Car dealership") owner, and a veteran football and basketball coach, Doc Stewart quickly became a popular figure across diverse segments of the university population. His oratory eloquence landed him an open job offer from the head of the UT English Department. After having coached the football team to an undefeated season, Stewart turned to implementing an entirely new style of basketball at Texas—one that emphasized ball movement and man-to-man defense and that essentially dispensed with the dribble altogether.[[43]](#cite_note-Penn_25-43) Texas entered the season expected to finish third or fourth in conference play behind [TCU](/wiki/TCU_Horned_Frogs_men%27s_basketball "TCU Horned Frogs men's basketball"), Oklahoma A&M, and possibly Texas A&M.[[43]](#cite_note-Penn_25-43) Texas opened the season with a one-point win and two four-point wins over Southwestern—an opponent that had lost its previous six games against the Longhorns by an average of almost 20 points—leading to concern in the local newspapers.[[43]](#cite_note-Penn_25-43) Contrary to prognostications, Texas opened the conference slate with two wins over Oklahoma A&M and sweeps of six other conference series to reach 14–0 in conference play, securing at least a share of the SWC championship with six games remaining, all away from Austin. The Longhorns next traveled more than 500 miles by train to open a long and bitter basketball rivalry with the [Arkansas Razorbacks](/wiki/Arkansas_Razorbacks_men%27s_basketball "Arkansas Razorbacks men's basketball"), then in their first year of competition in the sport, earning four- and 11-point wins in [Fayetteville](/wiki/Fayetteville,_Arkansas "Fayetteville, Arkansas").[[44]](#cite_note-Penn_26-44) Despite its unblemished record, Texas was still predicted to lose at least one game to the Aggies in College Station. The Longhorns instead managed 24–14 and 17–11 victories over the Aggies to finish as the last undefeated team in Texas and SWC history at 23–0. Senior guard [Abb Curtis](/wiki/Abb_Curtis "Abb Curtis") would later receive retroactive recognition for the 1924 season as UT's second-ever consensus first-team All-American in basketball.[[31]](#cite_note-NCAA_award_winners_2-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/1924_UTexas_basketball_team.jpg/330px-1924_UTexas_basketball_team.jpg)](/wiki/File:1924_UTexas_basketball_team.jpg)

E. J. "Doc" Stewart's undefeated 1924 Longhorn basketball team

Some have speculated that Stewart's devotion to his varied non-athletic interests was the root cause of his football and basketball teams' decline in performance over his tenure.[[39]](#cite_note-Texas_Football:_All-Time_Head_Coaches-39) Following the perfect 1924 season, Stewart's next three teams finished 17–8, 12–10, and 13–9. This slide—coupled with his football teams' similar decline in performance—resulted in the popular Stewart's controversial dismissal following the 1926–27 season.

#### 1927–36

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1927–36")]

Excepting two strong seasons—one particularly noteworthy—Texas maintained this level of relatively unremarkable performance in basketball for the better part of the next decade. Texas won only a single SWC Championship during the next nine seasons—in the exceptional 22–1 season of 1932–33, for which the Longhorns were retroactively named the season's top-ranked team by the [Premo-Porretta Power Poll](/wiki/Premo-Porretta_Power_Poll "Premo-Porretta Power Poll"). The university does not claim any title, and the NCAA does not recognize Premo-Porretta polls.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/MysteriousWalker.png/120px-MysteriousWalker.png)](/wiki/File:MysteriousWalker.png)

Fred Walker

[Fred Walker](/wiki/Mysterious_Walker "Mysterious Walker") (1927–31) coached the Longhorns following E.J. Stewart's dismissal, producing a 51–30 combined record during his four-year stint as head coach. Walker led Texas to an 18–2 overall record and 10–2 conference record during his second season. He was terminated following the Longhorns' disappointing 9–15 season in his fourth year.

[Ed Olle](/wiki/Ed_Olle "Ed Olle") (1931–34), who had played for Texas under Stewart, coached Texas for three seasons after Walker's dismissal, leading the Longhorns to a 22–1 overall mark and a conference championship during his second year. During his third year, Olle signaled that he would resign at the end of the season and recommended that freshman team and assistant varsity coach [Marty Karow](/wiki/Marty_Karow "Marty Karow") take his place.

Karow (1934–36) would direct Texas to a combined 31–16 record over his two years as head coach. His relationship with Texas Athletic Director and [Longhorn football](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_football "Texas Longhorns football") head coach [Jack Chevigny](/wiki/Jack_Chevigny "Jack Chevigny") marked by increasing friction, Karow resigned as head coach in the summer of 1936 and was hired shortly thereafter as the [baseball](/wiki/College_baseball "College baseball") head coach for the [United States Naval Academy](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Academy "United States Naval Academy").

### Jack Gray & H. C. "Bully" Gilstrap era (1937–51)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Jack Gray & H. C. \"Bully\" Gilstrap era (1937–51)")]

#### Pre-war Jack Gray years (1937–42)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Pre-war Jack Gray years (1937–42)")]

Only two seasons removed from his senior year at Texas, in which he earned consensus first-team All-American honors, and with only one year as an assistant coach with the Texas freshman team, the immensely popular [Jack Gray](/wiki/Jack_Gray_(basketball) "Jack Gray (basketball)") was hired as the fourteenth Texas men's basketball head coach in the summer of 1937 at the age of 25.[[46]](#cite_note-Little_18-46)

After his first two teams combined for a 24–21 record, Gray's 1938–39 team posted a 19–6 overall mark and won the [Southwest Conference](/wiki/Southwest_Conference "Southwest Conference") championship outright for UT's first basketball conference title in six years. The season featured the then-most anticipated intersectional matchup in school history, as [Phog Allen](/wiki/Phog_Allen "Phog Allen")'s [Kansas Jayhawks](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_men%27s_basketball "Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball") came to Austin.[[47]](#cite_note-Penn_57-47) The Jayhawks appeared to be on their way to winning the first game until the Longhorns rallied late in the second half for a 36–34 victory. The second game the following night proceeded more in line with expectations, with Kansas winning handily, 49–35. Following the series against KU, Texas traveled to [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City "Oklahoma City") to compete for the first time in the [All-College tournament](/wiki/All-College_Basketball_Classic "All-College Basketball Classic"), which had begun in 1937. The 1939 edition of the holiday tournament featured 32 teams from the Southwest and Midwest.[[47]](#cite_note-Penn_57-47) The Longhorns easily advanced through their tournament bracket, defeating [Southeastern State College](/wiki/Southeastern_Oklahoma_State_Savage_Storm "Southeastern Oklahoma State Savage Storm") ([Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma")), [Westminster College](/wiki/Westminster_College_(Missouri) "Westminster College (Missouri)") ([Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri")), [Kansas State Teachers College](/wiki/Emporia_State_Hornets_basketball "Emporia State Hornets basketball"), and tournament favorite [Baylor](/wiki/Baylor_Bears_men%27s_basketball "Baylor Bears men's basketball") before falling to [Central Missouri State Teachers College](/wiki/Central_Missouri_Mules_basketball "Central Missouri Mules basketball") in the championship game, 33–25. Texas began conference play with an upset loss to the [Rice Owls](/wiki/Rice_Owls_men%27s_basketball "Rice Owls men's basketball") before hosting the [Arkansas Razorbacks](/wiki/Arkansas_Razorbacks_men%27s_basketball "Arkansas Razorbacks men's basketball") for two games in Austin. The Longhorns won a close first contest, 41–37, before being thoroughly outclassed in the second, falling 65–41. With Texas reeling—having started 1–2 in SWC play, and hosting no conference games in Austin for the next month—the team's goal of ending the conference championship drought was in peril. The Longhorns began a four-game stretch of conference road games with a win over [Baylor](/wiki/Baylor_Bears_men%27s_basketball "Baylor Bears men's basketball") in [Waco](/wiki/Waco,_Texas "Waco, Texas") before continuing to [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas,_Texas "Dallas, Texas") to face the [SMU Mustangs](/wiki/SMU_Mustangs_men%27s_basketball "SMU Mustangs men's basketball"), who stood at 5–0 in conference play. Gray praised the Mustangs as "probably the most powerful team in the history of the school," and SMU coach [Whitey Baccus](/wiki/Whitey_Baccus "Whitey Baccus") confidently announced that his team would dispatch the Longhorns.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Instead, Texas handed SMU its first defeat of the conference season, 33–27. The Longhorns defeated the [TCU Horned Frogs](/wiki/TCU_Horned_Frogs_men%27s_basketball "TCU Horned Frogs men's basketball") and the [Texas A&M Aggies](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_Aggies_men%27s_basketball "Texas A&M Aggies men's basketball") in their remaining two conference road games before winning all five SWC contests in Austin, concluding with a 66–32 rout of the Aggies. With a nine-game conference winning streak, Texas had finished at 10–2 in SWC play to claim sole possession of the SWC championship. The Longhorns were one of eight teams to qualify for the [inaugural postseason NCAA tournament](/wiki/1939_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1939 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), where they fell 56–41 to the ["Tall Firs"](/wiki/1938%E2%80%9339_Oregon_Webfoots_men%27s_basketball_team "1938–39 Oregon Webfoots men's basketball team") of the [Oregon Webfoots](/wiki/Oregon_Ducks_men%27s_basketball "Oregon Ducks men's basketball") (later known as the Ducks), the eventual NCAA champion. Texas lost the West Regional third-place game to [Utah State](/wiki/Utah_State_Aggies_men%27s_basketball "Utah State Aggies men's basketball"), 51–49.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/3/39/Jack_Gray_%28coach_at_UTexas%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jack_Gray_(coach_at_UTexas).jpg)

Jack Gray, UT basketball All-American player (1932–35) and head coach (1936–42, 1945–51)

Hopes and expectations for the 1939–40 team were high, as all but one of the key players returned from the previous season's SWC champion and NCAA Tournament squad.[[49]](#cite_note-Penn_60-49) Texas opened the season with seven wins by an average of 18 points (and by no fewer than 11), including two wins over the [Texas Tech Red Raiders](/wiki/Texas_Tech_Red_Raiders_men%27s_basketball "Texas Tech Red Raiders men's basketball") in a home series that marked the first meetings between the two schools in basketball.[[50]](#cite_note-Fact_79-50) Gray was intent both upon raising national recognition of the program and upon toughening his team in the early part of the season for the conference slate ahead, and to both ends he sought to involve the Longhorns in intersectional competition against prominent teams in high-profile venues across the country.[[46]](#cite_note-Little_18-46)[[49]](#cite_note-Penn_60-49) [Ned Irish](/wiki/Ned_Irish "Ned Irish"), director of [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") and a pioneer in the promotion of college basketball in the 1930s, had invited Gray's Longhorns to play [Manhattan College](/wiki/Manhattan_Jaspers_basketball "Manhattan Jaspers basketball") as part of a doubleheader that included [Southern California](/wiki/USC_Trojans_men%27s_basketball "USC Trojans men's basketball") and [Long Island University](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn_Blackbirds_men%27s_basketball "LIU Brooklyn Blackbirds men's basketball").[[46]](#cite_note-Little_18-46) The UT Athletic Council agreed to fund the team's trip to New York for the contest, followed by a stop in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") to play [Temple](/wiki/Temple_Owls_men%27s_basketball "Temple Owls men's basketball"). In front of 18,425 fans, the Longhorns overwhelmed Manhattan by a score of 54–32, earning the praise of the New York sports media and basketball fans for their speed and the accuracy of their one-handed shooting, which Gray had popularized as a player and continued teaching as a coach.[[49]](#cite_note-Penn_60-49)[[51]](#cite_note-51) Continuing on to Philadelphia, Texas fell 47–37 to Temple, winners of the first [National Invitation Tournament](/wiki/1938_National_Invitation_Tournament "1938 National Invitation Tournament") two years prior.[[52]](#cite_note-Little_25-52) Having lost one of two road contests against Arkansas and a road game against SMU in overtime, Texas entered the penultimate game of the season at 18–3 and tied at 8–2 in conference play with the preseason conference favorite Rice Owls—a team that the Longhorns had defeated on Rice's home court earlier in the season, 50–46.[[53]](#cite_note-Penn_63-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Fact_83-54) In front of a raucous pro-Texas crowd of more than 8,000 fans packed into [Gregory Gymnasium](/wiki/Gregory_Gymnasium "Gregory Gymnasium"), the Longhorns suffered a one-point loss to the Owls, 42–41, to see their hopes of winning a second consecutive outright SWC championship dashed and their chances of even sharing the championship greatly diminished. Another painful defeat followed in the final game of the season, as the Longhorns fell to a 10–11 Texas A&M team in [College Station](/wiki/College_Station,_Texas "College Station, Texas"), 53–52, on a long running shot from the Aggies' backup center in the final seconds.[[52]](#cite_note-Little_25-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Penn_63-53) Texas finished 18–5 with no invitation to a postseason tournament. The Premo-Porretta Power Poll retroactively assigned the 1939–40 Longhorn team a national ranking of No. 17.

After his next two teams combined for a 28–19 overall record and a 12–12 record in conference play, Gray was notified of his acceptance for duty in the [Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") in April 1942, four months after the United States had entered the Second World War. Gray's assistant [Ed Price](/wiki/Ed_Price_(American_football) "Ed Price (American football)") had also left for naval service. [Longhorn football](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_football "Texas Longhorns football") assistant coach [Howard "Bully" Gilstrap](/wiki/Bully_Gilstrap "Bully Gilstrap") was appointed to coach the team for the duration of the war.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### Gilstrap as interim head coach (1942–45)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Gilstrap as interim head coach (1942–45)")]

In addition to both coaches, three starters from the 1941–42 team had departed for service in the war. Accordingly, expectations for the 1942–43 Longhorns were low.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Despite losses of coaches and players that were projected as insurmountable hardships, Texas defied expectations, winning 13 of its first 16 games. Gilstrap credited Gray and Price with encouragement and advice from afar and his players with a degree of cooperation he said he had not seen before. Gilstrap explained, "There were a lot of things I didn't know about the system, and the boys realized that. They came to the rescue. They've been assistant coaches as well as players. We've just been trying to work it out together."[[58]](#cite_note-58) After stumbling on a swing through [North Texas](/wiki/North_Texas "North Texas") late in the season with losses to TCU and SMU, the Longhorns concluded the regular season with victories over the [Baylor Bears](/wiki/Baylor_Bears_men%27s_basketball "Baylor Bears men's basketball") and Texas A&M to win a share of the SWC championship and qualify for the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/1943_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1943 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") for the second time. The Longhorns drew the Tournament co-favorite [Washington Huskies](/wiki/Washington_Huskies_men%27s_basketball "Washington Huskies men's basketball") for their first game.[[59]](#cite_note-Little_26-59) After falling behind by 13 points in the first half, Texas came back to win 59–55 behind 30 points from [John Hargis](/wiki/John_Hargis_(basketball) "John Hargis (basketball)") and 15 from Buck Overall to advance to its first-ever [Final Four](/wiki/Final_Four "Final Four"), where it drew the other Tournament co-favorite, the [Wyoming Cowboys](/wiki/Wyoming_Cowboys_men%27s_basketball "Wyoming Cowboys men's basketball").[[60]](#cite_note-Penn_70-60) It was then the Longhorns who surrendered an early 13-point lead, as the bigger and stronger Cowboys regrouped to win 58–54, on their way to defeating the [Georgetown Hoyas](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_men%27s_basketball "Georgetown Hoyas men's basketball") 46–34 for the NCAA championship.[[59]](#cite_note-Little_26-59)[[60]](#cite_note-Penn_70-60) Texas finished the season with a 19–7 overall record.

Following the 1943–44 and 1944–45 seasons, in which Gilstrap's Longhorn teams posted overall records of 14–11 and 10–10, respectively, Jack Gray returned as head coach with the end of the [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War") in August 1945.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

#### Post-war Jack Gray years (1945–51)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-war Jack Gray years (1945–51)")]

Gray took charge of a 1945–46 Texas team that returned only five lettermen—none of whom had ever played under him—and which had very little size, as both forward John Hargis and Robert Summers would be out for the entire season.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Little was expected of the Longhorns that season, but Texas managed to win its first seven games. The team's grave liabilities in defense and rebounding against bigger teams were never more evident that year than against defending—and soon-to-be-repeating—national champion [Oklahoma A&M](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Cowboys_basketball "Oklahoma State Cowboys basketball") (later renamed [Oklahoma State University](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_University "Oklahoma State University")) and its 7'0" All-American center, [Bob "Foothills" Kurland](/wiki/Bob_Kurland "Bob Kurland"). Kurland and the Aggies (later known as the Cowboys) dominated the diminutive Longhorns from start to finish, winning 69–34 in the opening round of the eight-team [All-College tournament](/wiki/All-College_Basketball_Classic "All-College Basketball Classic") in [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City "Oklahoma City"). The Longhorns dropped the second game of the tournament to fellow SWC member Rice, 55–52. The Longhorns opened a new season of SWC play with a road win over TCU. Texas was not expected to fare significantly better in two consecutive games against the towering Arkansas Razorbacks in [Fayetteville](/wiki/Fayetteville,_Arkansas "Fayetteville, Arkansas") than it had against Oklahoma A&M. The Longhorns acquitted themselves well in a close loss in the first game, 55–47, but the pre-game prognostications came to fruition the following night, as Arkansas routed Texas 90–63 in the second contest. After having lost four of five games, Texas posted an 8–3 record in its final 11 contests to finish with a respectable mark of 16–7 and a third-place conference finish, significantly exceeding preseason expectations for the undersized 1945–46 team.[[63]](#cite_note-Little_27-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Discussions had begun about the projected need to build a larger arena for UT basketball team.[[63]](#cite_note-Little_27-63) Longhorn basketball had grown significantly in popularity under Gray and Gilstrap's guidance.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Sellouts had not been particularly common during the war years, but the university was growing rapidly, and if Texas basketball continued to achieve success, a looming capacity problem was clearly foreseeable. Football and basketball were growing in popularity nationwide, and a spending and building boom was expected to take place in athletics departments around the country. No specific plans for basketball took shape at UT, but discussions of a larger gym or arena continued over the next three years.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Returning all but one top player and adding some military veterans and players from the freshman team, Gray's 1946–47 Texas team was thought to have a legitimate chance of winning the SWC championship, along with Arkansas, SMU, and defending SWC champion Baylor.[[67]](#cite_note-Penn_78-67) Future [Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") member and five-time [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") champion guard [Slater Martin](/wiki/Slater_Martin "Slater Martin") and forward John Hargis returned to the team to join guards Roy Cox and Al Madsen, who had returned the previous season. All four men, along with three other of that year's letterwinners, had served in the war. Martin, Cox, and Madsen were dubbed the "Mighty Mice."[[67]](#cite_note-Penn_78-67) Though the return of Hargis helped, the Longhorns were again a team not possessed of great size, but they did possess great speed and scoring ability. The Longhorns began the season with four blowout wins, the last and closest coming by a score of 46–34 against the Continental Air Liners of [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"). The game against the Air Liners was the last game Texas would play against a [semi-professional](/wiki/Semi-professional "Semi-professional") team, once a routine component of the nonconference schedule, until the 1955–56 season.[[67]](#cite_note-Penn_78-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) Not content to play only overmatched local teams for the remainder of the nonconference slate, Gray wanted to harden his team for the season ahead, and the Longhorns next embarked upon a 10-day, 4,000-mile train trip to face [Canisius](/wiki/Canisius_Golden_Griffins_men%27s_basketball "Canisius Golden Griffins men's basketball") in [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Long Island](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn_Blackbirds_men%27s_basketball "LIU Brooklyn Blackbirds men's basketball") in New York City, and [DePaul](/wiki/DePaul_Blue_Demons_men%27s_basketball "DePaul Blue Demons men's basketball") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[67]](#cite_note-Penn_78-67) Texas defeated Canisius 52–46 before traveling to New York for the most-anticipated contest of the trip, the game in [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") against [Clair Bee](/wiki/Clair_Bee "Clair Bee")'s LIU Blackbirds, who were averaging 90 points per game and had recently defeated defending national champion Oklahoma A&M. In front of a strongly pro-LIU crowd of 18,453, the Longhorns upset the Blackbirds, 47–46. Texas next traveled to Chicago to face the DePaul Blue Demons of [Ray Meyer](/wiki/Ray_Meyer "Ray Meyer"), whose team had won the [NIT](/wiki/1945_National_Invitation_Tournament "1945 National Invitation Tournament") two years earlier, and won the final game of their road trip in a rout, 61–43.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Before returning to Austin, the 7–0 Longhorns stopped in Oklahoma City to play in the All-College Tournament. Texas dominated the [Missouri Tigers](/wiki/Missouri_Tigers_men%27s_basketball "Missouri Tigers men's basketball") 65–46 before falling to Oklahoma A&M, the two-time defending national champion, in the semifinal by a single point, 40–39. The Longhorns defeated the [Oklahoma Sooners](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners_men%27s_basketball "Oklahoma Sooners men's basketball") in the third-place game the following night by a score of 62–50.[[70]](#cite_note-Penn_79-70) Texas was only occasionally challenged during the remainder of the regular season, winning its three remaining nonconference games by 29, 24, and 12 points and seven of its first 10 conference games by 12 or more points (and the first 10 SWC contests by an average of 16.6 points).[[71]](#cite_note-71) The Longhorns entered the final weekend of the conference season needing only one win in two games against the second-place Razorbacks. In front of more than 8,000 fans at Gregory Gym, Arkansas led for most of the first game before Slater Martin led a late surge to secure the win and the outright conference championship for Texas, 49–44.[[72]](#cite_note-Penn_81-72) The pressure to win the SWC championship thus relieved, the Longhorns easily dispatched the Razorbacks the following night, 66–46, to finish the regular season 24–1 overall and 12–0 in SWC play for their first undefeated conference season since Doc Stewart's 1923–24 team finished 23–0. Texas traveled to [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") to face Wyoming in the first game of the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/1947_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1947 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"). Four players from each team had been on the 1943 teams that faced off in the Final Four on Wyoming's way to the NCAA championship.[[72]](#cite_note-Penn_81-72) Texas trailed until the final minutes of the second matchup, and Martin's long shot with 35 seconds remaining provided the margin of victory, with the Longhorns winning 42–40 to advance to the Final Four for the second time, where they would face Oklahoma. Despite having defeated the Sooners earlier in the season by 12 points, the Longhorns trailed 53–49 in the final minute of their second contest. Texas scored five points to take a 54–53 lead with seconds remaining, but OU scored on a 40-foot shot as time expired to defeat the Longhorns, 55–54.[[72]](#cite_note-Penn_81-72) Texas returned to Madison Square Garden to play the [City College of New York](/wiki/CCNY_Beavers_men%27s_basketball "CCNY Beavers men's basketball") in the national third-place game prior to the NCAA championship game between OU and [Holy Cross](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Crusaders_men%27s_basketball "Holy Cross Crusaders men's basketball") (won by the Crusaders, 58–47).[[72]](#cite_note-Penn_81-72) Texas defeated CCNY 54–50 to finish the season with 26 wins and two last-second, one-point defeats.[[54]](#cite_note-Fact_83-54)

With demand for tickets outstripping the [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of Gregory Gym, calls began to grow louder for the construction of a new arena. At the same time, a group of Austin businessmen announced plans for the construction of a 10,000-seat arena adjacent to soon-to-be-built Interregional Highway, the precursor to [Interstate 35](/wiki/Interstate_35 "Interstate 35"), and 23rd Street and East Avenue—plans which ultimately did not bear fruit.[[70]](#cite_note-Penn_79-70) During the following season, members of the UT Development Board met with several dozen prominent alumni to discuss plans for the construction of a 20,000-seat coliseum, at a cost of roughly $2 million, to be located south of [Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Darrell_K_Royal%E2%80%93Texas_Memorial_Stadium "Darrell K Royal–Texas Memorial Stadium"). UT architects had already begun to draw up designs for such an arena, but the effort did not progress beyond the planning stages.[[73]](#cite_note-Penn_83-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Slater_Martin_%281%29.jpeg/250px-Slater_Martin_%281%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Slater_Martin_(1).jpeg)

Slater Martin, UT first-team All-American and five-time NBA champion, in Minneapolis Lakers uniform, c. 1953

Slater Martin and Al Madsen returned to the 1947–48 Longhorn team, among others, but this team was short on depth compared to the previous season's Final Four team, with only seven players in Gray's rotation. For the first time in six years, freshmen were barred from playing on the varsity team.[[74]](#cite_note-Penn_82-74) Texas started the season 6–0—highlighted by a 51–42 win over the [Texas Tech Red Raiders](/wiki/Texas_Tech_Red_Raiders_men%27s_basketball "Texas Tech Red Raiders men's basketball") and a 51–30 blowout of the [LSU Tigers](/wiki/LSU_Tigers_men%27s_basketball "LSU Tigers men's basketball"), who proved too slow to handle the speed of another fast and quick Longhorn team—before embarking on another road trip to the Northeast, stopping in New York for the third time in two seasons. In a rematch of the previous season's national third-place game, Texas faced the CCNY Beavers in Madison Square Garden. Texas surrendered an 18-point first-half lead but withstood a late CCNY rally, holding on to win, 61–59.[[74]](#cite_note-Penn_82-74) Texas defeated the [St. Joseph's Hawks](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Hawks_men%27s_basketball "Saint Joseph's Hawks men's basketball") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), 61–57, before returning to Oklahoma City for the All-College Tournament. There the Longhorns defeated the [Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets](/wiki/Georgia_Tech_Yellow_Jackets_men%27s_basketball "Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets men's basketball") by a score of 54–45 and the [Alabama Crimson Tide](/wiki/Alabama_Crimson_Tide_men%27s_basketball "Alabama Crimson Tide men's basketball"), 40–31, to advance to the title game against Oklahoma A&M. For the second consecutive year, the Aggies defeated the Longhorns by a single point, 32–31, after [Bob Harris](/wiki/Bob_Harris_(basketball) "Bob Harris (basketball)") again provided the winning margin in the final five seconds.[[73]](#cite_note-Penn_83-73) Texas opened conference play 5–0, pushing its overall record to 16–1, before suffering three consecutive losses to Baylor, Rice, and Arkansas to see its prospects for defending its SWC crown dashed. The Longhorns recovered to win the second game against the Razorbacks in their weekend trip to Fayetteville, 54–43, to halt the losing streak. By the time of the Longhorns' next contest, against Baylor, the Bears stood at 11–0 in conference play and had already secured the SWC championship. Although Texas could do no better than second place, more than 8,000 fans squeezed into 7,500-seat Gregory Gym to see the Longhorns hand the Bears their only defeat of the conference season, 32–29, after Al Madsen added a layup and a free throw in the final 20 seconds.[[75]](#cite_note-Penn_84-75) The win over Baylor landed Texas an invitation to the [1948 NIT](/wiki/1948_National_Invitation_Tournament "1948 National Invitation Tournament") with two conference games remaining.[[75]](#cite_note-Penn_84-75) (Baylor would go on to advance to the championship game of the [1948 NCAA tournament](/wiki/1948_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1948 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), where the Bears fell to [Adolph Rupp](/wiki/Adolph_Rupp "Adolph Rupp")'s [Kentucky Wildcats](/wiki/Kentucky_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball") in the first NCAA championship game appearance for either program.) Texas narrowly avoided an upset loss to SMU at home before blowing out Texas A&M in College Station, 54–34, to finish 9–3 and in second place in the SWC play. The Longhorns boarded a train for New York the following morning to face the favored [Violets](/wiki/NYU_Violets_men%27s_basketball "NYU Violets men's basketball") of [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), led by future fourth overall [1948 draft](/wiki/1948_BAA_draft "1948 BAA draft") pick, 16-year NBA star, and Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame member [Dolph Schayes](/wiki/Dolph_Schayes "Dolph Schayes"). Martin and Madsen led Texas to a 43–39 lead with under four minutes remaining—after Texas had trailed by seven points midway through the second half—but NYU tied the game in the final minute and scored the final basket on a long shot with six seconds remaining to win the quarterfinals game, 45–43.[[75]](#cite_note-Penn_84-75) The Longhorns finished the season 20–5, marking the first time in program history that Texas had won 20 or more games in consecutive seasons.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)

### Texas recedes from the national stage (1951–76)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Texas recedes from the national stage (1951–76)")]

After two losing seasons during the program's first five years, Texas suffered only one losing season from 1912 to 1950, reaching two Final Fours and one Elite Eight during the first decade of the NCAA Tournament. The Longhorns would finish with losing records ten times from 1951 to 1976.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)

#### Abrupt decline (1951–59)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Abrupt decline (1951–59)")]

[Thurman "Slue" Hull](/w/index.php?title=Thurman_%22Slue%22_Hull&action=edit&redlink=1 "Thurman \"Slue\" Hull (page does not exist)") was hired as men's basketball head coach prior to the 1951–52 season. In his five seasons as the Texas head coach, Hull led the Longhorns to one Southwest Conference championship (1953–54) and finished with an overall record of 60–58 (.508). He was dismissed following the 1955–56 season after his final two teams produced a combined record of 16–32—easily the worst two-year period in the history of Longhorn basketball to that point. Hull was the first Texas coach since [W. E. Metzenthin](/wiki/W._E._Metzenthin "W. E. Metzenthin"), who coached the basketball team for three years during the program's first five seasons (1909–11), to finish with a Texas career win percentage below .600.[[11]](#cite_note-Fact_65-11)

Following Hull's dismissal, [Marshall Hughes](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Hughes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marshall Hughes (page does not exist)") was hired as the next men's basketball head coach prior to the 1956–57 season. Under Hughes, the Texas basketball program reached the nadir of its existence. Hughes was fired after only three seasons—each with a losing record, and each worse than the one preceding it—with an overall record of 25–46 (.352) after his final team posted a mark of just 4–20, tying the 1954–55 season as the then-worst in program history and concluding a five-year span of futility in which the Longhorns produced an overall record of 41–78 (.345).[[76]](#cite_note-76)

#### Uneven recovery (1959–67)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Uneven recovery (1959–67)")]

Between coaches [Harold Bradley](/wiki/Harold_Bradley_(basketball) "Harold Bradley (basketball)"), hired as head coach in 1959, and [Leon Black](/wiki/Leon_Black_(basketball) "Leon Black (basketball)"), who directed the basketball team from 1967 to 1976, the Longhorns played in four [NCAA Tournaments](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), two under each coach, as a result of winning the [Southwest Conference](/wiki/Southwest_Conference "Southwest Conference") five times (three times outright) in 17 years.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)

In Bradley's first season, the Longhorns won the SWC outright to reach the [1960 NCAA tournament](/wiki/1960_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1960 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), where they fell to the [Kansas Jayhawks](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_men%27s_basketball "Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball") by a score of 80–71 in the Sweet Sixteen contest. Texas subsequently lost the Midwest Regional third-place game to [DePaul](/wiki/DePaul_Blue_Demons_men%27s_basketball "DePaul Blue Demons men's basketball") by a score of 67–61. Texas finished the season ranked No. 13 in the [UPI](/wiki/United_Press_International "United Press International") [Coaches Poll](/wiki/Coaches_Poll#College_basketball "Coaches Poll"), marking the first time that the basketball team had finished the season ranked since the introduction of the [AP poll](/wiki/AP_Poll#College_basketball "AP Poll") and the Coaches Poll for the 1948–49 and 1950–51 seasons, respectively.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Bradley's 1962–63 team again won the SWC outright and reached 20 wins for the first time since [Jack Gray](/wiki/Jack_Gray_(basketball) "Jack Gray (basketball)")'s 1947–48 Longhorns. Texas advanced to the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/1963_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1963 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") and defeated the [Texas Western Miners](/wiki/UTEP_Miners_basketball "UTEP Miners basketball") by a score of 65–47 in its opening game to advance to the Sweet Sixteen, where the Longhorns fell 73–68 to [Ed Jucker](/wiki/Ed_Jucker "Ed Jucker")'s defending two-time national champion and fifth-consecutive Final Four participant [Cincinnati Bearcats](/wiki/Cincinnati_Bearcats_men%27s_basketball "Cincinnati Bearcats men's basketball").[[78]](#cite_note-78) Texas would go on to win the Midwest Regional third-place game against future Texas head coach [Abe Lemons](/wiki/Abe_Lemons "Abe Lemons")' [Oklahoma City Chiefs](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Stars_men%27s_basketball "Oklahoma City Stars men's basketball") by a score of 90–81. The Longhorns finished the season ranked No. 12 in the Coaches Poll.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

The 1964–65 Longhorns tied SMU for the conference championship but lost the tiebreaker for the conference's [NCAA tournament](/wiki/1965_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1965 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") berth and thus did not participate in postseason play. In the following two seasons, Bradley's Texas teams posted overall records of 12–12 and 14–10. Bradley retired following the 1966–67 season.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-Penn_142-81) He finished with an overall record of 123–75 (.631) and a conference record of 73–39 (.651) as Texas head coach.[[11]](#cite_note-Fact_65-11)

#### Resumed decline (1967–76)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Resumed decline (1967–76)")]

With the hiring of [Leon Black](/wiki/Leon_Black_(basketball) "Leon Black (basketball)") prior to the 1967–68 season, Texas entered a period that saw the reversal of most of its progress since the lost decade of the 1950s. Black opened with three losing seasons and one non-winning season before his 1971–72 team finished 19–9, won a share of the conference championship, and reached the [1972 NCAA tournament](/wiki/1972_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1972 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament").[[82]](#cite_note-Fact_88-82) The Longhorns defeated the [Houston Cougars](/wiki/Houston_Cougars_men%27s_basketball "Houston Cougars men's basketball") (who had been approved for SWC membership in 1971, but did not play a conference basketball schedule until 1975–76) 85–74 to advance to the Sweet Sixteen, where they fell to the [Kansas State Wildcats](/wiki/Kansas_State_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Kansas State Wildcats men's basketball") by a score of 66–55. Texas also lost the regional third place game 100–70 to [Southwestern Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_Ragin%27_Cajuns_men%27s_basketball "Louisiana Ragin' Cajuns men's basketball") (now the [University of Louisiana at Lafayette](/wiki/University_of_Louisiana_at_Lafayette "University of Louisiana at Lafayette"), branded "Louisiana" for intercollegiate athletics), but that game, plus all Ragin' Cajun tournament games in 1972 and [1973](/wiki/1973_NCAA_University_Division_basketball_tournament "1973 NCAA University Division basketball tournament"), were scrubbed from NCAA records when USL was handed a two-year [death penalty](/wiki/Death_penalty_(NCAA) "Death penalty (NCAA)") in August 1973 (the game vs. USL was not a forfeit win for Texas).

After posting a 13–12 overall record in 1972–73, Black's Longhorns recorded three consecutive losing seasons, each with fewer wins and more defeats than the one before. Black's 1973–74 team managed to win the SWC championship outright, even with an overall record of 12–15, and advanced to the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/1974_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1974 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), where the Longhorns fell to the [Creighton Bluejays](/wiki/Creighton_Bluejays_men%27s_basketball "Creighton Bluejays men's basketball") in the first round, 77–61.[[82]](#cite_note-Fact_88-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Following 10–15 and 9–17 seasons in 1974–75 and 1975–76, respectively, Black resigned from his position as Texas head coach.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Black finished with an overall record of 106–121 (.467) and a record of 63–65 (.492) in conference play.[[11]](#cite_note-Fact_65-11) Prior to Black, only two Texas head coaches had finished with overall losing records—[W. E. Metzenthin](/wiki/W._E._Metzenthin "W. E. Metzenthin") (1909–11) and Marshall Hughes (1956–59)—and each had only coached for three seasons.[[11]](#cite_note-Fact_65-11) Black coached for nine seasons, only twice finishing with a winning record.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)

### Abe Lemons years (1976–82)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Abe Lemons years (1976–82)")]

Following Leon Black's resignation, Texas Athletic Director and Longhorn head football coach [Darrell Royal](/wiki/Darrell_Royal "Darrell Royal") selected then-[University of Texas-Pan American](/wiki/Texas%E2%80%93Pan_American_Broncs_men%27s_basketball "Texas–Pan American Broncs men's basketball") and former longtime [Oklahoma City University](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Stars_men%27s_basketball "Oklahoma City Stars men's basketball") head coach [Abe Lemons](/wiki/Abe_Lemons "Abe Lemons") as his primary target for the open position.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Lemons and fellow Oklahoman Royal agreed to a five-year contract worth roughly $30,000 per year,[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-Penn_173-87) and Lemons was subsequently introduced as the twentieth Longhorn head basketball coach in the program's 72 seasons.

Thanks to his exuberant personality, quick and acerbic wit, and rare quote-making skill,[[88]](#cite_note-Shot-88)[[89]](#cite_note-Lemons_Gets_the_Boot-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92) the [cigar](/wiki/Cigar "Cigar")-smoking Abe Lemons' growing status as a [fan](/wiki/Fan_(person) "Fan (person)") favorite anticipated any of his notable accomplishments in basketball at Texas.[[93]](#cite_note-Basket_Cases-93) Though he and his staff inherited two players that would play central roles on his most successful Texas teams in freshman [forward](/wiki/Forward_(basketball) "Forward (basketball)") and Los Angeles high school player of the year [Ron Baxter](/wiki/Ron_Baxter "Ron Baxter") and [sophomore](/wiki/Sophomore_year "Sophomore year") [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn_Tigers_men%27s_basketball "Auburn Tigers men's basketball") transfer [Jim Krivacs](/wiki/Jim_Krivacs "Jim Krivacs"),[[87]](#cite_note-Penn_173-87) Lemons was nevertheless assuming control of a moribund program coming off of three consecutive losing seasons, the last of which featured the then-third-most defeats in the history of the program.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9) Lemons was less than sanguine about the Longhorns' prospects for the 1976–77 season. Asked in a preseason media session if he felt his first Texas team to be worthy of a top-twenty ranking, Lemons replied, "You mean in the state?"[[94]](#cite_note-94) After starting the season with a 6–9 record, Texas managed a six-game winning streak against some of the conference's weaker teams before stumbling to a 1–4 finish over the final five games. Despite Lemons' dejected mood following the final game of the season,[[95]](#cite_note-95) a loss to [Baylor](/wiki/Baylor_Bears_men%27s_basketball "Baylor Bears men's basketball") in the final men's basketball game in [Gregory Gymnasium](/wiki/Gregory_Gymnasium "Gregory Gymnasium"), his first team had posted a four-game improvement in its season record over the 9–17 squad of the prior year, finishing 13–13 on the season.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)

No significant preseason expectations attended the [1977–78 Texas Longhorns](/wiki/1978_Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball_season "1978 Texas Longhorns men's basketball season"), a team that would produce one of the more successful seasons in Longhorn basketball history.[[96]](#cite_note-Penn_178-96) After a one-point loss in the opening game against [Southern California](/wiki/USC_Trojans_men%27s_basketball "USC Trojans men's basketball") in Los Angeles, Texas inaugurated the $37-million, 16,231-seat [Special Events Center](/wiki/Frank_Erwin_Center "Frank Erwin Center") with an 83–76 victory over the [Oklahoma Sooners](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners_men%27s_basketball "Oklahoma Sooners men's basketball"), the first of eight straight wins.[[96]](#cite_note-Penn_178-96)[[97]](#cite_note-Fact_89-97) Texas lost 65–56 to fifth-ranked, defending national champion [Marquette](/wiki/Marquette_Golden_Eagles_men%27s_basketball "Marquette Golden Eagles men's basketball") before posting another nine straight victories, including a 75–69 upset of third-ranked and eventual [Final Four](/wiki/Final_Four "Final Four") participant [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas_Razorbacks_men%27s_basketball "Arkansas Razorbacks men's basketball"), with its famed "Triplets" (guards [Sidney Moncrief](/wiki/Sidney_Moncrief "Sidney Moncrief"), [Ron Brewer](/wiki/Ron_Brewer "Ron Brewer"), and [Marvin Delph](/wiki/Marvin_Delph "Marvin Delph")). The win over [Eddie Sutton](/wiki/Eddie_Sutton "Eddie Sutton")'s Razorbacks vaulted Lemons' Longhorns to a No. 15 ranking in the Associated Press Poll, Texas' first appearance in the poll since a one-week showing at No. 20 in 1949, the inaugural season of the AP basketball poll.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) Texas would finish the regular season ranked No. 12 in the AP poll with records of 22–4 overall and 14–2 in conference play, sharing the [Southwest Conference](/wiki/Southwest_Conference "Southwest Conference") Championship with the Razorbacks.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Despite the impressive season, Texas saw its hopes of playing in the 32-team [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") dashed in a two-point loss to [Houston](/wiki/Houston_Cougars_men%27s_basketball "Houston Cougars men's basketball") in the [SWC Tournament Final](/wiki/Southwest_Conference_men%27s_basketball_tournament "Southwest Conference men's basketball tournament"). Houston claimed the [automatic bid](/wiki/Automatic_bid "Automatic bid") to the Tournament, Arkansas received an [at-large bid](/wiki/At-large_bid "At-large bid"), and the Longhorns were left to accept a bid to the [1978 National Invitation Tournament](/wiki/1978_National_Invitation_Tournament "1978 National Invitation Tournament").[[101]](#cite_note-101) Texas would storm through the tournament to reach the NIT Championship Game against the [North Carolina State Wolfpack](/wiki/North_Carolina_State_Wolfpack_men%27s_basketball "North Carolina State Wolfpack men's basketball"), defeating [Temple](/wiki/Temple_Owls_men%27s_basketball "Temple Owls men's basketball"), [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska_Cornhuskers_men%27s_basketball "Nebraska Cornhuskers men's basketball"), and [Rutgers](/wiki/Rutgers_Scarlet_Knights_men%27s_basketball "Rutgers Scarlet Knights men's basketball") by an average of over 17 points in the first three rounds. The Longhorns posted an easy 101–93 victory over the Wolfpack to win the NIT Championship behind 22, 26, and 33 points, respectively, from [point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard") [Johnny Moore](/wiki/Johnny_Moore_(basketball) "Johnny Moore (basketball)") and 1978 NIT Co-MVPs Ron Baxter and Jim Krivacs.[[97]](#cite_note-Fact_89-97)[[102]](#cite_note-102) After the end of the 1977–78 season, Abe Lemons was named National Coach of the Year by the [National Association of Basketball Coaches](/wiki/National_Association_of_Basketball_Coaches "National Association of Basketball Coaches"). Lemons remains the only men's basketball coach in UT history to earn National Coach of the Year honors.[[20]](#cite_note-Top_100_Moments-20)[[103]](#cite_note-103)

With its four leading scorers returning, Texas entered the [1978–79 season](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_season "1978–79 NCAA Division I men's basketball season") with a No. 6 ranking in the AP poll and as the near-unanimous favorite to win the SWC championship.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The Longhorns struggled early, beginning the season 7–4 and falling out of the AP rankings after a 21-point road defeat to [Bill Cartwright](/wiki/Bill_Cartwright "Bill Cartwright") and the [San Francisco Dons](/wiki/San_Francisco_Dons_men%27s_basketball "San Francisco Dons men's basketball").[[97]](#cite_note-Fact_89-97) After another blowout road loss to [Texas Tech](/wiki/Texas_Tech_Red_Raiders_men%27s_basketball "Texas Tech Red Raiders men's basketball"), Texas regrouped to win three straight road games and 12 of its next 13 games, including a three-point upset of 10th-ranked Arkansas in [Fayetteville](/wiki/Fayetteville,_Arkansas "Fayetteville, Arkansas"), a 23-point blowout of [Shelby Metcalf](/wiki/Shelby_Metcalf "Shelby Metcalf")'s No. 15 [Texas A&M Aggies](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_Aggies_men%27s_basketball "Texas A&M Aggies men's basketball") in Austin, and an eight-point win over the 11th-ranked Aggies three weeks later in [College Station](/wiki/College_Station,_Texas "College Station, Texas").[[97]](#cite_note-Fact_89-97) During the preceding thirty seasons, Texas had only managed a total of six wins against AP-ranked opponents, and never more than one such victory in a single year.[[105]](#cite_note-105) A home loss to 14th-ranked Arkansas was the lone blemish during the 13-game stretch, a game that featured a shouting and shoving episode, famous in SWC lore, between Lemons and Eddie Sutton after Sutton had admonished Texas player Johnny Moore on the court. Police and assistant coaches intervened, but Lemons told the media following the game that if Sutton dared to address his players again, he would "tear his Sunday clothes" and "liquidate his a\*\*."[[106]](#cite_note-106) Struggling [SMU](/wiki/SMU_Mustangs "SMU Mustangs") dealt Texas a shocking defeat in the final game of the regular season, depriving the Longhorns of sole possession of the SWC crown and forcing them to share the conference championship with Arkansas for the second straight season.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Following a 39–38 loss to the ninth-ranked Razorbacks in the SWC Tournament Final, Texas received a No. 4 seed and a bye to the second round in the [1979 NCAA tournament](/wiki/1979_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1979 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"). Texas fell to No. 5-seeded Oklahoma in the tournament to finish the season with a 21–8 overall record and a No. 15 final ranking in the [UPI](/wiki/United_Press_International "United Press International") [Coaches' Poll](/wiki/Coaches%27_Poll "Coaches' Poll").[[97]](#cite_note-Fact_89-97) The Longhorns drew an average of 15,886 fans per home game in 1978–79, a school and Erwin Center record that to this point has not been challenged.[[108]](#cite_note-Fact_123-108)

The 1979–80 Texas Longhorns returned only one starter, forward Ron Baxter.[[109]](#cite_note-Penn_188-109) [LaSalle Thompson](/wiki/LaSalle_Thompson "LaSalle Thompson"), 6'10" [center](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)") and future Longhorn great, joined the program as a freshman.[[109]](#cite_note-Penn_188-109) Texas ended the regular season with an 18–10 overall record and a 10–6 conference record,[[97]](#cite_note-Fact_89-97) finishing third behind Texas A&M and Arkansas in SWC play. Passed over by the NCAA Tournament [selection committee](/wiki/NCAA_basketball_tournament_selection_process "NCAA basketball tournament selection process"), Texas received a bid to the [1980 NIT](/wiki/1980_National_Invitation_Tournament "1980 National Invitation Tournament"), the last postseason tournament a Lemons-coached Texas team would reach.[[110]](#cite_note-Fact_89–90-110) The Longhorns posted a 70–61 win over [St. Joseph's](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Hawks_men%27s_basketball "Saint Joseph's Hawks men's basketball") before falling to [Southwestern Louisiana](/wiki/Southwestern_Louisiana_University "Southwestern Louisiana University"), 77–76, in the second round to finish with a 19–11 overall record. Baxter, the 1980 [Southwest Conference Player of the Year](/wiki/Southwest_Conference_Men%27s_Basketball_Player_of_the_Year "Southwest Conference Men's Basketball Player of the Year"),[[111]](#cite_note-Fact_141-111) finished his UT career as the then-all-time school leader in both scoring and [rebounding](/wiki/Rebound_(basketball) "Rebound (basketball)").[[112]](#cite_note-112)

The 1980–81 Longhorn team carried little in the way of preseason expectations of success. Even before the season began, the program was embroiled in controversy and turmoil. Lemons had summarily fired assistant Steve Moeller, leading to a caustic public feud between the two men, with each blaming the other for recent disappointing [recruiting](/wiki/Recruiting_(college_athletics) "Recruiting (college athletics)") results. Moeller charged that Lemons' lack of inhibition with regard to public and private criticism of players was damaging the program. Only one of the four players signed in the 1981 class—6'9" forward [Mike Wacker](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Wacker&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mike Wacker (page does not exist)")—was considered a coveted prospect. Texas opened with a home loss to [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Tigers_men%27s_basketball "Pacific Tigers men's basketball"). The regular season's zenith, a two-point win over Arkansas in Fayetteville on January 12, did nothing to reverse the team's downward trajectory, with losses to [TCU](/wiki/TCU_Horned_Frogs "TCU Horned Frogs"), SMU, [North Texas](/wiki/North_Texas_Mean_Green_men%27s_basketball "North Texas Mean Green men's basketball"), [Rice](/wiki/Rice_Owls_men%27s_basketball "Rice Owls men's basketball") following shortly thereafter. The Longhorns stumbled to a 10–14 overall record with two conference games remaining. Lemons' habitual sarcasm and indiscriminately acid tongue, heretofore endearing to fans if not academic administrators, began to draw criticism, with some citing his routinely quippish comments as evidence that he failed to take his team's poor performance sufficiently seriously.[[113]](#cite_note-Penn_195-113) Nonetheless, just as Lemons began to face notable fan frustration and criticism for the first time at Texas, his team began an unexpected run of late-season success. Lemons' team managed to win the two remaining regular season games as well as three of four games in the SWC Tournament—including a 76–73 victory over No. 15 Arkansas in the semifinal round—to avoid finishing with a losing record. The end-of-season success quelled discontent for the time being, with fans and [commentators](/wiki/Sports_commentator "Sports commentator") pointing to the return of LaSalle Thompson, Mike Wacker, and a healthier and more experienced supporting cast as reason for renewed confidence about the near future and optimism about the program's prospects.[[113]](#cite_note-Penn_195-113)
