## Polls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Polls")]

Texas has been ranked in at least one of the final AP or coaches polls in 21 seasons since their introduction prior to the 1948–49 and 1950–51 seasons, respectively. The Longhorns have recorded seven top-ten finishes and three top-five finishes in one or more of the final polls. Ten of Rick Barnes' teams, five of Tom Penders' teams, two of Abe Lemons' teams, and two of Harold Bradley's teams finished the season ranked.[[9]](#cite_note-Fact_66-9)[[208]](#cite_note-208) As of April 1, 2025, Texas teams have been ranked in 318 of 1300 total weekly AP Polls (24.5%) since the inception of the poll in the 1948–49 season (31st all-time in AP Poll appearances), and in 361 of 1286 total weekly coaches polls (28.1%) since the inception of the poll in the 1950–51 season.[[209]](#cite_note-209)

### All-time series records against non-Big 12 former SWC members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: All-time series records against non-Big 12 former SWC members")]

In series against former [Southwest Conference](/wiki/Southwest_Conference "Southwest Conference") members who are not current members of the Big 12, Texas does not trail any opponent.

In contests against these opponents since the dissolution of the Southwest Conference, Texas holds the lead against all opponents but SMU, whom the Longhorns have not played since joining the Big 12. The Longhorns hold the advantage against every opponent in the last five games played and in the last ten games played against each respective opponent.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Big 12 rivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Big 12 rivals")]

#### Oklahoma Sooners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Oklahoma Sooners")]

With the formation of the [Big 12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference") in 1996, the [Oklahoma Sooners](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners_men%27s_basketball "Oklahoma Sooners men's basketball") became the Longhorns' main rival in basketball. Texas and Oklahoma are not traditional rivals in any sport other than football, due to their prior residence in different conferences (UT in the Southwest Conference and OU in the [Big Eight Conference](/wiki/Big_Eight_Conference "Big Eight Conference")); nonetheless, the two basketball programs had already compiled a significant series history prior to formation of the Big 12, having met 14 times from 1947 to 1957, 11 times from 1986 until the beginning of Big 12 competition in 1997, and at various other times in the programs' histories. The Sooners entered the Big with a 29–13 lead in the all-time series, having won 10 of the 11 games played from 1986 to 1996.[[211]](#cite_note-Fact_75-211) In the Big 12, the competitiveness of the Texas and OU basketball programs—which are second and third in all-time Big 12 regular season conference wins, respectively—only accelerated the development of the basketball rivalry.[[212]](#cite_note-2014–15_Big_12_Media_Guide_80-212)

The Texas Longhorns and the Oklahoma Sooners play one game in [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") and one game in [Norman](/wiki/Norman,_Oklahoma "Norman, Oklahoma") each year during the regular season. Oklahoma leads the overall series 54–39; Texas leads 26–25 in games played since the inception of the Big 12. Texas has held the upper hand in recent years, winning 23 of 35 meetings from 2003 to 2018.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[211]](#cite_note-Fact_75-211)

#### Kansas Jayhawks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Kansas Jayhawks")]

Rick Barnes' tenure also coincided with the emergence of a rivalry between Texas and the [Big 12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference")'s traditional national basketball powerhouse, the [Kansas Jayhawks](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_men%27s_basketball "Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball").[[213]](#cite_note-213)[[214]](#cite_note-214)[[215]](#cite_note-215) Under Rick Barnes, the Longhorns emerged as Kansas's most consistent competition for Big 12 Conference supremacy. From the inception of the Big 12 through February 18, 2018, Texas has produced a 223–139 (.616) mark against conference competition during the regular season, trailing only Kansas's record of 299–63 (.826).[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[212]](#cite_note-2014–15_Big_12_Media_Guide_80-212)[[216]](#cite_note-216)

The first decade of the 2000s saw the peak of this new rivalry, with the Longhorns at one point trailing Kansas only 5–7 in games played over an almost-nine-season period, and with many of the contests being closely contested classics. Since the beginning of the 2008–09 season, Texas has been less competitive both nationally and in the rivalry, trailing Kansas 2–15 in games played since that time.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[217]](#cite_note-Fact_73-217)

Texas and Kansas played one game during the regular season until the 2011–12 season, when the two schools began meeting twice per year. Kansas leads the overall series 30–8 and has won 26 of the 33 contests since the Big 12 was formed. Current KU head coach [Bill Self](/wiki/Bill_Self "Bill Self") is 20–6 in games against Texas since becoming the Jayhawks' head coach prior to the 2003–04 season.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[217]](#cite_note-Fact_73-217)

#### Baylor Bears

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Baylor Bears")]

The Longhorns' series with the [Baylor Bears](/wiki/Baylor_Bears_basketball "Baylor Bears basketball") has featured more games played than any other UT basketball series; it is also the conference series most lopsided in UT's favor, with the exception of the Longhorns' series against former Southwest Conference opponent [Rice](/wiki/Rice_Owls_men%27s_basketball "Rice Owls men's basketball") and the much more abbreviated series against former Big 12 member [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska_Cornhuskers_men%27s_basketball "Nebraska Cornhuskers men's basketball"). Texas leads 162–90 in the overall series with Baylor.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[218]](#cite_note-Fact_71–72-218)

The intensity of the in-state rivalry has grown sharply in recent years with Baylor's emergence as a nationally competitive program under current head coach [Scott Drew](/wiki/Scott_Drew "Scott Drew").[[219]](#cite_note-219)[[220]](#cite_note-220) Through the end of the 2008–09 regular season, Texas held a 25–3 record against Baylor in Big 12 competition and a 24–0 record against the Bears under Rick Barnes. In games played since the [2009 Big 12 conference tournament](/wiki/2009_Big_12_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2009 Big 12 men's basketball tournament") semifinals, Baylor holds a 15–7 edge over Texas.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[218]](#cite_note-Fact_71–72-218)

#### Texas Tech Red Raiders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Texas Tech Red Raiders")]

Texas and [Texas Tech](/wiki/Texas_Tech_Red_Raiders_basketball "Texas Tech Red Raiders basketball") have played at least twice yearly since the 1957–58 season. The Longhorns and the Red Raiders play one game in Austin and one game in [Lubbock](/wiki/Lubbock,_Texas "Lubbock, Texas") each year during the regular season.

At the time that UT and Texas Tech entered the Big 12, Texas Tech held a 49–45 lead in the all-time series against Texas. Longtime Red Raider coach [Gerald Myers](/wiki/Gerald_Myers "Gerald Myers")' teams dominated the Longhorns for much of the 1970s and 1980s, with the only interlude of Longhorn success occurring during the six-year tenure of Abe Lemons (1976–82), who finished 8–4 against the Red Raiders.[[221]](#cite_note-221) By the time Tom Penders became the Texas head coach prior to the 1988–89 season, Texas Tech held a 43–32 lead in the series. Penders narrowed Texas Tech's lead to 50–49 during his tenure (1988–98). During Rick Barnes' 17 seasons as head coach (1998–2015), the Longhorns posted a 33–4 record against the Red Raiders, for a period of dominance unequaled at any other point in the series.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[50]](#cite_note-Fact_79-50)

Texas currently leads the overall series 86–57 and has won 41 of the 49 contests since the formation of the Big 12.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[50]](#cite_note-Fact_79-50)

#### Oklahoma State Cowboys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Oklahoma State Cowboys")]

The formation of the Big 12 led to the development of a competitive rivalry between Texas and the [Oklahoma State Cowboys](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Cowboys_basketball "Oklahoma State Cowboys basketball"), two programs that had already compiled a significant series history despite their residence in different conferences since OSU's departure from the Southwest Conference following the 1924–25 season. Between 1941 and 1979, Texas and OSU met 32 times, with the Cowboys winning 21 of the meetings. At the time of the formation of the Big 12, OSU held a 24–19 lead in the all-time series.[[211]](#cite_note-Fact_75-211)

The rivalry reached its peak in the early years of the Big 12, above all when the tenures of Rick Barnes and [Eddie Sutton](/wiki/Eddie_Sutton "Eddie Sutton") coincided at the two schools, a time when both programs were nationally prominent. From the 1999–2000 season through the 2004–05 season, at least one of the two teams was ranked in 13 of 14 total contests, and both teams were ranked at the time of eight of the 14 meetings.[[222]](#cite_note-222) In recent years, the rivalry has declined in intensity as both programs have receded from the national spotlight.

Texas and Oklahoma State have played at least twice yearly since 1997, the first Big 12 basketball season. The Longhorns and the Cowboys play one game in Austin and one game in [Stillwater](/wiki/Stillwater,_Oklahoma "Stillwater, Oklahoma") each year during the regular season. Texas currently leads the overall series 49–43 and holds a 30–19 lead in games played in Big 12 competition.[[6]](#cite_note-Cumulative_Stats-6)[[211]](#cite_note-Fact_75-211)

### Other rivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Other rivals")]

#### Arkansas Razorbacks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Arkansas Razorbacks")]

Texas and [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas_Razorbacks_men%27s_basketball "Arkansas Razorbacks men's basketball") shared a bitter rivalry in basketball—stoked by the mutual enmity that characterized their [primary rivalry in football](/wiki/Arkansas%E2%80%93Texas_football_rivalry "Arkansas–Texas football rivalry")—throughout most of the existence of the Southwest Conference. Until Arkansas' departure for the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference") after the 1990–91 season, the Longhorns and the Razorbacks had played at least twice—and often three times—each year since the 1924 season. Texas and Arkansas each won or shared 22 [SWC championships](/wiki/List_of_Southwest_Conference_champions "List of Southwest Conference champions") (nine more than the nearest competitor, SMU)—although Texas claimed seven of these in the years before Arkansas established its basketball program in 1924 and in the years after Arkansas left the conference.

The Texas–Arkansas basketball rivalry was particularly competitive in its first quarter-century, and both programs enjoyed substantial overall success during this time, with each appearing in two Final Fours and one Elite Eight with the beginning of the NCAA Tournament in 1939 through the end of the 1940s. Arkansas led the series 29–25 over this span.[[223]](#cite_note-Fact_71-223)

Both Texas and Arkansas faded from national significance with the end of the 1940s. From the 1949–50 season through the 1975–76 season, Arkansas won only two SWC championships, made only a single NCAA Tournament appearance, and posted an overall record of 312–338 (.480).[[224]](#cite_note-224) Texas fared modestly better during this time, winning seven SWC championships, making four NCAA Tournament appearances, and posting an overall record of 342–323 (.514).[[11]](#cite_note-Fact_65-11) The Longhorns and Razorbacks split their conference series during this period, with each winning 29 games.[[223]](#cite_note-Fact_71-223)

The rivalry grew especially heated from the mid-1970s onward as both schools' athletic departments poured additional resources into their basketball programs, both for coaching hires and for facilities, each seeking to revive programs that had receded from the national stage over the preceding quarter-century. While both Texas and Arkansas appeared to be on the verge of establishing nationally competitive programs with the hires of Abe Lemons and Eddie Sutton, respectively, only Arkansas managed to achieve sustained success in the following years, winning at least 20 games in 13 seasons from 1977 to 1991, with Sutton (1974–85) and [Nolan Richardson](/wiki/Nolan_Richardson "Nolan Richardson") (1985–2002) each reaching a Final Four and an Elite Eight. (Richardson would lead the Razorbacks to two more Final Fours and a national championship after Arkansas' departure from the SWC.) Arkansas dominated the series over this final phase of the SWC rivalry, winning 27 out of 37 games played during this period.[[223]](#cite_note-Fact_71-223)

Arkansas led the series 85–64 upon leaving the SWC; the Razorbacks currently lead the all-time series 87–68, with Texas having won four of the six games played since Arkansas joined the SEC.[[223]](#cite_note-Fact_71-223)

#### Texas A&M Aggies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Texas A&M Aggies")]

See also: [Lone Star Showdown](/wiki/Lone_Star_Showdown "Lone Star Showdown")

Before the [Texas A&M Aggies](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_Aggies "Texas A&M Aggies")' [departure](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9312_Southeastern_Conference_realignment "2010–12 Southeastern Conference realignment") for the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference") at the end of the 2012 [academic year](/wiki/Academic_year "Academic year"), Texas and [Texas A&M](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_Aggies_men%27s_basketball "Texas A&M Aggies men's basketball") had played at least twice (and up to four times) yearly since 1917. During their concurrent membership in the Big 12, the Longhorns and the Aggies played two games during the regular season, with the venue alternating between the home courts of each school. Texas leads the overall series 137–86.[[225]](#cite_note-225)

By the time the Big 12 was formed, the in-state rivalry was at a low ebb. The Longhorns had won 18 of the previous 21 contests against the Aggies, dating back to the 1987–88 season. Texas continued to dominate the series in Big 12 play, winning 15 of the first 16 contests between the schools in their new conference.[[211]](#cite_note-Fact_75-211) By the time the Aggies hired [Billy Gillispie](/wiki/Billy_Gillispie "Billy Gillispie") prior to the 2004–05 season, the lopsided nature of the series—and the lack of Aggie fan interest in a program that had gone ten consecutive years without a winning season or postseason appearance under prior coaches—had long since diminished the stature of the once-heated rivalry.

With the rejuvenation of the Texas A&M basketball program under [Billy Gillispie](/wiki/Billy_Gillispie "Billy Gillispie") (2004–07) and [Mark Turgeon](/wiki/Mark_Turgeon "Mark Turgeon") (2007–11), the basketball rivalry between Texas and Texas A&M quickly intensified. The Aggies successfully defended their home court from Gillispie's first year through the 2009–10 season, Mark Turgeon's second-to-last season with A&M. The Longhorns defeated the Aggies in all three games in Turgeon's final season and in both games in [Billy Kennedy](/wiki/Billy_Kennedy_(basketball) "Billy Kennedy (basketball)")'s first season with the Aggies. Texas is 12–7 against Texas A&M since Gillispie was hired in 2004.[[211]](#cite_note-Fact_75-211)

The rivalry will be renewed when Texas [joins A&M in the SEC](/wiki/History_of_the_Big_12_Conference#2021 "History of the Big 12 Conference") prior to the 2024–25 season.

### Retired numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Retired numbers")]

Main article: [List of NCAA men's basketball retired numbers](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_men%27s_basketball_retired_numbers "List of NCAA men's basketball retired numbers")

Three Longhorn men's basketball players have had their Texas jersey numbers retired: [Slater Martin](/wiki/Slater_Martin "Slater Martin"), [T. J. Ford](/wiki/T._J._Ford "T. J. Ford"), and [Kevin Durant](/wiki/Kevin_Durant "Kevin Durant").

The primary requirement for the retirement of a UT student-athlete's number is that the individual have won a widely recognized [National Player of the Year award](/wiki/List_of_U.S._men%27s_college_basketball_national_player_of_the_year_awards "List of U.S. men's college basketball national player of the year awards").[[226]](#cite_note-Retired_numbers-226) Former UT Athletic Director [DeLoss Dodds](/wiki/DeLoss_Dodds "DeLoss Dodds") stated that an exception was made in the case of [Slater Martin](/wiki/Slater_Martin "Slater Martin") because, as a [Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") inductee, he was a legend in the sport of basketball but had competed before any of the widely recognized awards were established (the [*Sporting News* Men's College Basketball Player of the Year](/wiki/Sporting_News_Men%27s_College_Basketball_Player_of_the_Year "Sporting News Men's College Basketball Player of the Year") was established in 1943, but it was not awarded from 1947 to 1949).[[226]](#cite_note-Retired_numbers-226)[[227]](#cite_note-227)

#### National Player of the Year

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: National Player of the Year")]

[T. J. Ford](/wiki/T._J._Ford "T. J. Ford") and [Kevin Durant](/wiki/Kevin_Durant "Kevin Durant") are the only Texas players to win one or more of the widely recognized [National Player of the Year](/wiki/List_of_U.S._men%27s_college_basketball_national_player_of_the_year_awards "List of U.S. men's college basketball national player of the year awards") awards. Ford won the [Naismith Trophy](/wiki/Naismith_College_Player_of_the_Year "Naismith College Player of the Year")[[228]](#cite_note-228) and the [Wooden Award](/wiki/John_R._Wooden_Award "John R. Wooden Award")[[229]](#cite_note-229)—as well as four of the other eight widely recognized awards[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[231]](#cite_note-231)[[232]](#cite_note-232)[[233]](#cite_note-233)—in 2003 as a sophomore. Durant was the unanimous national player of the year in 2007 as a true freshman, winning all 10 awards.[[234]](#cite_note-234)[[235]](#cite_note-235)[[236]](#cite_note-236)[[237]](#cite_note-237)[[238]](#cite_note-238)[[239]](#cite_note-239)[[240]](#cite_note-240)[[241]](#cite_note-241) Durant was the first freshman ever to win any of the National Player of the Year awards.[[242]](#cite_note-242)[[243]](#cite_note-243)

The [Bob Cousy Award](/wiki/Bob_Cousy_Award "Bob Cousy Award") is awarded annually by the [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") to the top [point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard") in NCAA Division I men's basketball. Augustin") is the only Longhorn to win the Bob Cousy Award.[[244]](#cite_note-Fact_139-244)

#### Perry Wallace Most Courageous Award

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Perry Wallace Most Courageous Award")]

The [Perry Wallace](/wiki/Perry_Wallace "Perry Wallace") Most Courageous Award is the men's version of the [USBWA Most Courageous Award](/wiki/USBWA_Most_Courageous_Award "USBWA Most Courageous Award"), presented annually by the [United States Basketball Writers Association](/wiki/United_States_Basketball_Writers_Association "United States Basketball Writers Association") to one or more individuals (not necessarily players) who, in the organization's words, have "demonstrated extraordinary courage reflecting honor on the sport of amateur basketball."[[245]](#cite_note-245) First presented in 1978 as a single award, separate men's and women's awards were first presented in 2010. The award's bifurcation by sex or gender does not reflect that of the recipient, but is instead based on whether the recipient was involved with the men's or women's game. This award was originally not restricted to college basketball, but every recipient since 1980 has been associated with the college game. The men's award was renamed in 2021 in memory of Wallace, the first [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") to play basketball in the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference").[[246]](#cite_note-246) [Andrew Jones](/wiki/Andrew_Jones_(basketball) "Andrew Jones (basketball)") is the first Longhorn to receive this award; he shared 2022 honors with Justin Hardy, a player at [NCAA Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") [Washington (MO)](/wiki/Washington_University_Bears "Washington University Bears"). Both averaged double figures in scoring for their respective teams in 2021–22 while battling cancer (leukemia for Jones, stomach cancer for Hardy).[[247]](#cite_note-247)

#### All-America honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: All-America honors")]

Twenty Texas basketball players have received [All-America honors](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Basketball_All-Americans "NCAA Men's Basketball All-Americans") on 26 occasions; six have been recognized as All-Americans in two different seasons. Seven Longhorns have received [consensus first-team All-America honors](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Basketball_All-Americans#Consensus_teams "NCAA Men's Basketball All-Americans"), and three others have received consensus second-team All-America honors. Thirteen Texas players have received first-team All-America honors in 14 seasons, with one Longhorn player having been selected as a first-team All-American twice.[[248]](#cite_note-248)

### Professional basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Professional basketball")]

#### NBA draft history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: NBA draft history")]

As of July 30, 2021, 48 Longhorn men's basketball players have been selected in the [NBA draft](/wiki/NBA_draft "NBA draft") in the history of the program. Of these, 19 were selected in the first round, and 13 were selected in the second round. Among Big 12 programs, Texas trails only Kansas (33) in the number of players drafted in the first round all-time.

Since 1997, Texas has had 23 players drafted overall; of these, 15 were drafted in the first round. Among Big 12 programs, Texas trails only Kansas (34 picks overall, 22 first-round picks) in the numbers of players drafted overall and in the first round during this period. The 15 Longhorn players drafted in the first round from 1997 to 2021 exceed the all-time numbers of first-round picks at each Big 12 school but Kansas.

### Olympians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: Olympians")]

Four Longhorn men's basketball players have competed in the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") in men's basketball on six occasions, with three players winning [gold medals](/wiki/Gold_medal "Gold medal").[[298]](#cite_note-Fact_153-298)[[299]](#cite_note-Durant_2016-299)

Kevin Durant was the leading scorer on the [United States men's basketball team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_basketball_team "United States men's basketball team") that won the gold medal in the [2012 Olympics](/wiki/2012_Olympics "2012 Olympics"). Durant's 156 total points during the Olympic tournament set a record for most points scored by an American basketball player in Olympic competition, surpassing the record previously set by [Spencer Haywood](/wiki/Spencer_Haywood "Spencer Haywood") in the [1968 Olympics](/wiki/1968_United_States_men%27s_Olympic_basketball_team "1968 United States men's Olympic basketball team") by 11 points. Durant also set the U.S. record for most made three-point shots in an Olympic competition with 34 (on 65 attempts). In addition to leading the U.S. team in scoring, he finished second on the team in rebounding, first in blocked shots, and second in steals.[[298]](#cite_note-Fact_153-298)

In the [2016 Olympics](/wiki/2016_Olympics "2016 Olympics"), Durant again led the U.S. men's team in scoring with 155 total points—one point fewer than his U.S. Olympic record from 2012—and in minutes played. He finished second on the team in steals, third in assists, and fourth in rebounding. Durant converted 25 three-point shots on only 43 attempts (.581).[[299]](#cite_note-Durant_2016-299)

### Coaching honors and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Coaching honors and awards")]

#### National Coach of the Year honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: National Coach of the Year honors")]

Abe Lemons (1976–82) won the [NABC](/wiki/National_Association_of_Basketball_Coaches "National Association of Basketball Coaches") [National Coach of the Year award](/wiki/NABC_Coach_of_the_Year "NABC Coach of the Year") following his [second season](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball_team "1977–78 Texas Longhorns men's basketball team") at Texas, a season that saw the Longhorns finish 26–5, win a share of the Southwest Conference title, and win the [1978 NIT Championship](/wiki/1978_National_Invitation_Tournament "1978 National Invitation Tournament").[[298]](#cite_note-Fact_153-298) Texas had finished just 9–17 two years earlier in Leon Black's last season as head coach.

[Rodney Terry](/wiki/Rodney_Terry "Rodney Terry") was named [Sporting News National Coach of the year](/wiki/Sporting_News_Men%27s_College_Basketball_Coach_of_the_Year_Award "Sporting News Men's College Basketball Coach of the Year Award") for the [2022–23 season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_season "2022–23 NCAA Division I men's basketball season"), becoming the first Texas coach to win the award since it was introduced in 1964.[[300]](#cite_note-300)

#### John R. Wooden Legends of Coaching Award

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: John R. Wooden Legends of Coaching Award")]

The [Wooden Legends of Coaching Award](/wiki/John_R._Wooden_Award#Legends_of_Coaching_Award "John R. Wooden Award") is a lifetime achievement award established prior to the 1998–99 season. Selection for the award depends upon a number of on- and off-court factors, from coaching success and philosophy to evidence of good character to graduation rates. Rick Barnes won the Wooden Legends of Coaching Award following the 2008–09 season.[[301]](#cite_note-301)

#### Conference Coach of the Year honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Conference Coach of the Year honors")]

Abe Lemons was recognized as the Southwest Conference Coach of the Year for the 1977–78 season, the season that saw him win the NABC National Coach of the Year award. Tom Penders received Southwest Conference Coach of the Year honors in 1994 and 1995, and Rick Barnes was named [Big 12 Coach of the Year](/wiki/Big_12_Conference_Men%27s_Basketball_Coach_of_the_Year "Big 12 Conference Men's Basketball Coach of the Year") in 1999, 2003, 2008, and 2014.[[111]](#cite_note-Fact_141-111)

#### District-level Coach of the Year honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=79 "Edit section: District-level Coach of the Year honors")]

Rick Barnes won recognition as NABC District 9 Coach of the Year in five seasons (1999, 2001, 2003, 2008, 2014). Barnes was also a four-time recipient of [USBWA](/wiki/USBWA "USBWA") District VII Coach of the Year honors (1999, 2001, 2003, 2011).[[302]](#cite_note-Fact_33-302)[[303]](#cite_note-303)[[304]](#cite_note-304)

## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Records")]

See also: [Texas Longhorns men's basketball statistical leaders](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_men%27s_basketball_statistical_leaders "Texas Longhorns men's basketball statistical leaders")

Team records include single-season, single-game, and NCAA tournament records for the Texas program. Program records for individual players include career totals and averages as well as single-season totals and averages.

All records are current as of the end of the [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_season "2014–15 NCAA Division I men's basketball season").[[305]](#cite_note-305)
